title: The Farm-Woman's Winter
content:
I

    If seasons all were summers,
     And leaves would never fall,
    And hopping casement-comers
     Were foodless not at all,
    And fragile folk might be here
     That white winds bid depart;
    Then one I used to see here
     Would warm my wasted heart!

II

    One frail, who, bravely tilling
     Long hours in gripping gusts,
    Was mastered by their chilling,
     And now his ploughshare rusts.
    So savage winter catches
     The breath of limber things,
    And what I love he snatches,
     And what I love not, brings.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Thomas Hardy, titled "The Farm-Woman's Winter", represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
